content.text: Leaked internal messages from Shaw indicated that Eliza Labs was ceasing all development and operations due to a deteriorating market, treasury trending toward zero, and inability to reach revenue targets. Core developer Odilitime confirmed the April 12 message was legitimate internal communication. Community members expressed confusion about whether ElizaOS as a project was shutting down entirely. Odilitime and other contributors clarified that while Eliza Labs as a funded organization was scaling back and stopping payments to some dedicated developers, the ElizaOS open source project itself was continuing. Contributions were described as rising, an agent had been deployed to help merge and clean up issues, version 1.x had been stable, and version 2.x was in active development. Shaw stated he intended to continue working on Eliza as an open source community project. A suggestion was raised about converting the project to a DAO structure. Stan later clarified that Labs was an experimental organization for sub-projects, and the current refocus is on the core framework itself, with other Labs projects described as on hold rather than abandoned.

A heated community debate broke out over the project's direction across multiple days. Community members accused Shaw and the team of making false promises, failing to deliver revenue, and poor communication. Others pushed back, confirming that ElizaOS v3 is almost ready and the team is actively working on the Milady app and Eliza app. Multiple community members urged the team to publish a roadmap on the ElizaOS X account to build momentum and trust, especially given favorable market conditions. Odilitime indicated the X account update was in Twitter's hands. Community members also noted the official X account had been inactive for nearly three weeks. Token underperformance relative to the broader crypto market rally was a recurring frustration, with Odilitime acknowledging the token was up on a 24-hour basis and encouraging celebrating small wins.

Persistent scam activity was reported across the community throughout the week. Fake airdrop posts with everyone tags were identified and confirmed as scams by Odilitime and moderators. A user named frog.cs was repeatedly called out as a scammer. A fake Solana airdrop impersonating Odilitime was flagged. A user named NovaOS was banned after sending fake links. Scammers also impersonated community member kenk in the arena channel, with moderators confirming the accounts were banned. Odilitime warned that scammers were attempting to impersonate him and asked to be informed of any such instances. A bot called migrate-helper was noted as running a security check on his behalf. A security researcher also reported finding multiple vulnerabilities in the ElizaOS open source application and asked about a bug bounty program. Odilitime confirmed there is no bug bounty program but offered to receive details via direct message, and the researcher ultimately disclosed the issues.

Community member satsbased shared tweets from shawmakesmagic explaining the background behind the ai16z token migration. According to the posts, the migration was forced because the original platform refused to implement voting features, was uncooperative in private communications, and dumped 5 percent of the token supply on holders, ultimately causing significant harm to the project. A new user who missed the migration deadline asked about converting Ai16z tokens to Eliza, and Odilitime confirmed migration is now closed with no workaround available. Community members also confirmed that staking is currently not available for ElizaOS tokens.

In the framework development channel, Stan submitted a pull request to the plugin-anthropic repository adding OAuth authentication allowing LLM calls to be routed through a Claude Max subscription instead of API keys, and missing TEXT_REASONING model type handlers. Odilitime reviewed and merged the PR after leaving comments on two issues. A build error was encountered related to a missing workspace dependency and a browser build failure involving an unresolved jsonrepair module. Stan pushed a follow-up PR to address it and mentioned plans to add streaming support. Odilitime also shared PR 6733 titled feat: pipeline hooks, introducing a pipeline hook subsystem in the elizaos/core package with registerPipelineHook and applyPipelineHooks with typed support, described as useful for the DPE prompt optimizer and plugin-typography.

Several ecosystem and commerce-related discussions took place during the week. The CROO Network V1 Pioneers Program was introduced, offering developers a CLI-based SDK that auto-generates an ERC-4337 account abstraction wallet, allows agent registration, service listing, and enables automated on-chain payment settlement between agents, with USDT rewards for the first 100 participants. Metaplex announced Agent Tokens on Solana featuring instant global trading, hundreds of integrated agents, and $50,000 in MPLX rewards. A developer discussed payment infrastructure for agents on Solana using the x402 protocol, with Stan suggesting supporting both USDC-SPL on Solana via PayAI and Base USDC. Community member oceanrun shared a new npm package called @oceanrun/trustgate implementing ERC-8004 trust scoring for x402 agents. The Orbis API was highlighted for its AI Agent Memory API providing persistent memory across sessions, along with a referral program paying 25 percent of referred developer payments in USDC.

The week's development began with implementation of a new utils/batch-queue system designed to centralize concurrency, retry logic, and task storage, supporting embedding drains, action-filter index builds, and knowledge embedding paths. Comprehensive dependency updates were carried out including cargo group updates across 21 directories covering packages such as bytes, quinn-proto, and rustls-webpki, as well as npm_and_yarn group updates across 11 directories including drizzle-orm in the agent package.

A major focus mid-week was stabilizing the CI/CD pipeline across multiple repositories. The team resolved widespread release workflow failures and race conditions caused by concurrent jobs and non-fast-forward push conflicts by implementing serialization, retry logic, and build fixes across the elizaos/eliza, elizaos-plugins/plugin-anthropic, and elizaos-plugins/registry repositories. Release reliability was further improved by ignoring dirty plugin submodules during publishing. The Anthropic plugin received OAuth support for Claude Max subscriptions and a new CLI-based headless authentication mode. Core stability improvements included a legacy fallback for agent restarts and new diagnostics for monitoring plugin state drift. Two new release failures were reported for versions v2.0.0-alpha.160 and v2.0.0-alpha.162.

Plugin ecosystem stability and security improvements were a focus on April 15. For the Discord plugin, PR 48 introduced safer default configurations, DM allowlist gating, and generation timeout fallbacks. For the Telegram plugin, PR 27 implemented a fix to prevent duplicate pollers per bot token. PR 341 was opened against the plugin registry to add the at-thecolony/elizaos-plugin. On April 16, linting processes in the Ollama plugin were standardized by replacing relative biome paths with bunx invocations. The megaswap-elizaos-plugin was submitted to the registry pending approval.

On April 17, three pull requests were opened awaiting review: a submission to plugin-evm adding Radius Network support with chain identifier eip155:723487, and two plugin registry submissions for @thecolony/elizaos-plugin and megalaunch-elizaos-plugin. On April 18, a large batch of dependency updates was submitted covering Supabase and Postgres docker tags updated to v17.6.1.108, @coral-xyz/borsh bumped to ^0.32.0, Capacitor monorepo updated to v8.3.1, Uniswap v2 and v3 SDKs, @types/node updated to v25.6.0, gymnasium updated to ~=1.2.3, and Android Gradle build tools updated to v8.13.2. A Discord chat UX improvement was submitted removing heartbeat messages and bumping the orchestrator pin. A new plugin @quantoracle/plugin-quantoracle was proposed for the registry, and a new issue was opened proposing Merxex integration for agent-to-agent commerce.

On April 19, subagent synthesis was refined by implementing end_turn reading and removing the task-heartbeat module. The InMemoryDatabaseAdapter was improved to handle accurate memory updates when roomId changes occur. Discord routing and submodule dependencies were updated for plugin-agent-orchestrator and plugin-cron. A new plugin called plugin-hiveexchange was introduced to enable agent-native prediction markets. Two additional plugins are pending registry addition: elizaos-plugin-deepblue and plugin-elizaos. Two new proposals were submitted as open issues around integrating Merxex to facilitate commerce between autonomous agents.
